planting.note: Sow seeds in early spring in lean, sharply drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; sea thrift is extremely salt-tolerant and wind-resistant — ideal for coastal gardens where many other plants struggle. Deadhead spent flower stems promptly to extend the bloom season and prevent the grass-like foliage from becoming bare in the center. Divide clumps every 3–4 years to maintain vigor.

directions: Plant in lean, sharply drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; sea thrift is a circumboreal coastal wildflower — a salt-spray survivor of sea cliffs and rocky shores across the Northern Hemisphere — forming dense, rounded cushions of narrow, grass-like, deep-green leaves (evergreen to semi-evergreen) and producing from spring through early summer (with possible rebloom in fall) spherical, papery, bright-rose-pink to deep-pink or white flower heads on stiff, upright stems of 6–12 inches, each rounded head tightly packed with tiny florets. The papery bracts persist decoratively as the flowers fade, extending the display. Salt tolerance is exceptional — truly one of the best plants for seaside gardens exposed to ocean spray and saline winds. It thrives in the same difficult conditions as thyme and lavender: lean, dry, well-drained soils in full sun with little competition. A classic edging plant for seaside cottage gardens, rock gardens, and gravel paths. The cultivar 'Dusseldorf Pride' (deep rose-pink) and 'Alba' (pure white) are widely available.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost date, or sow directly in spring; blanket flower is short-lived (often 2–4 years) in humid climates with heavy soil — sharp drainage is critical to longevity. Deadhead regularly to prolong the extended bloom season. The plant often self-sows, producing seedlings that may vary from the parent.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to alkaline soil in full sun; blanket flower is a North American prairie hybrid producing some of the most vivid and eye-catching daisy flowers in horticulture — large (3–4 inch), wheel-like blooms in the most intense combinations of red, orange, gold, and yellow, with the traditional pattern showing scarlet-red ray petals broadly tipped in rich golden-yellow and a dark, burgundy-red central disk, creating a warm-toned, richly colored flower that earns the name 'blanket flower' from its resemblance to the bold geometric patterns of Native American blankets. The bloom season is extraordinarily long — from early summer through hard frost — with regular deadheading maintaining a near-continuous display. Drought-tolerant and heat-loving, it thrives in the exact difficult conditions (poor soil, heat, dry spells) that exhaust other perennials, making it outstanding for low-maintenance sunny borders. Modern cultivars include the compact 'Goblin' (12 inches), 'Fanfare' (tubular petals), and 'Arizona Sun' (deep red with gold tips). Short-lived in clay or humid conditions — excellent drainage is the key to perenniality.

planting.note: Plant corms 3–4 inches deep in moist, moderately fertile, well-drained soil in full sun to part shade in spring; montbretia spreads rapidly by cormlets along the underground stolons and can become invasive in mild, moist climates — it has naturalized extensively on the coasts of Ireland, Wales, and the Pacific Northwest. In zones 6–7 mulch corms heavily in winter or lift them like gladiolus for frost-free storage.

directions: Plant corms in moist, fertile, well-drained soil in full sun to part shade; common montbretia is a Victorian-era hybrid (Crocosmia aurea x C. pottsii) that has become one of the most naturalizing of all summer-blooming South African corm plants, spreading by chains of cormlets to form large, grassy colonies and producing, in mid to late summer, arching, branched sprays of funnel-shaped, intense orange-red flowers that bend in graceful, one-sided racemes above fans of broad, sword-shaped leaves — creating one of the most characteristic and cheerful sights of the late-summer garden. The foliage, in pleated fans of bright lime-green, is ornamental from spring through autumn. The flowers, individually tubular and slightly flared, are irresistible to hummingbirds. Modern Crocosmia hybrids and cultivars derived from this cross and other species include the famous 'Lucifer' (flame-red, 4 feet, zones 5–9), 'Emily McKenzie' (large orange flowers with dark mahogany blotch), 'George Davidson' (golden-yellow), and 'Solfatare' (bronze foliage, yellow flowers). The species hybrid naturalized so freely in coastal Ireland and Wales that it has become part of the wild landscape.

planting.note: Sow seeds in early spring or direct-sow in fall; mountain cornflower spreads by underground rhizomes and self-sows freely — it can be invasive in some regions and should be deadheaded before seeds ripen to limit spread. After the main late-spring flush, shear plants back hard to encourage a fresh crop of foliage and often a second flush of bloom in late summer.

directions: Plant in moderately fertile, well-drained, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in full sun to light shade; mountain cornflower is the perennial version of the annual bachelor's button — a stout, spreading perennial from European mountain meadows producing large (2–3 inch), spidery, brilliant violet-blue to deep blue-violet cornflower heads with deeper violet centers and contrasting purplish-maroon outer florets, displayed on upright stems above soft, gray-green, woolly foliage from late spring through early summer with a possible second flush in late summer if cut back hard after the first flowering. The cobalt-blue and violet tones are among the truest and most saturated blues available in early-summer perennials, and the large, spidery flower heads have a more romantic, wildflower quality than the more refined blue of delphiniums. White ('Alba'), pink ('Carnea'), and bicolor cultivars exist but the species violet-blue is generally considered the most ornamental. Spreads vigorously and is potentially aggressive in fertile soils; more restrained in lean, dry conditions. Self-sows freely and can naturalize rapidly — an asset in a wildflower garden, a management concern in a formal border.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; Caucasian pincushion flower requires alkaline to neutral soil (pH 6.5–7.5) — acidic soils cause poor performance and early death. It is longer-lived in cool climates (zones 3–6) than in hot, humid conditions and may require division every 3–4 years to maintain vigor. Deadhead continuously for a long blooming season from summer through frost.

directions: Plant in well-drained, alkaline to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun; Caucasian pincushion flower is a classic cottage-garden perennial from the Caucasus Mountains producing lavender-blue to soft violet, 2–3 inch, pincushion-like flower heads on long, wiry stems from midsummer through fall — each flower head composed of a central dome of tubular disc florets surrounded by a ring of enlarged, ruffled outer florets in soft lavender to lilac-blue to near-white, held 18–24 inches high on slender stems that sway gracefully in garden breezes, making them exceptional as long-stemmed cut flowers. The very long vase life and delicate, informal character of the flowers has made this one of the premier cottage-garden and cutting-garden perennials since the Victorian era, appearing in countless English cottage-garden paintings alongside sweet peas and roses. Requires alkaline to neutral soil — plants in acid conditions perform poorly and decline. Cultivars include 'Clive Greaves' (medium lavender-blue, one of the oldest named forms still widely sold), 'Miss Willmott' (white), and 'Perfecta' (lavender-blue, frilled florets).
